topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room hummed, a high, irritating whine that seemed to amplify the frantic thumping in my chest. I stared at the roster tacked to the corkboard, the names of my supposed teammates blurred by a rising tide of nausea. They were all there, except for me. A cold sweat prickled along my spine, despite the stuffy air. Every glance from the coach felt like a spotlight, dissecting my inadequacy. I clutched the worn strap of my duffel bag so tightly my knuckles turned white. When the coach finally called my name, the sound echoed unnervingly loud in the silence. The ensuing silence before the announcement stretched into an eternity, each second a hammer blow against my composure. When the word 'cut' finally landed, the world tilted. My legs felt suddenly weak, rooted to the cold tile floor. I just nodded, a jerky, involuntary movement, and turned, the heavy thud of my exit echoing the sudden emptiness inside me.

[story 2]

My hands were slick with a clammy perspiration as I reviewed the final team sheet. The paper felt flimsy, a cruel mockery of the solid confidence I’d built over years of grueling practice. A knot tightened in my stomach, a hard, painful lump that refused to loosen. I kept replaying the final drills in my mind, searching for the misstep, the moment I’d faltered. When the coach finally approached, his expression was neutral, which somehow made the impending pronouncement feel infinitely worse. "We've made our final selections," he stated, his voice flat. My breath hitched. I forced myself to meet his gaze, trying to project an assurance I absolutely did not feel. The reality of being left out settled over me like a heavy shroud, making the air feel thin and hard to draw in. I managed a tight, brittle smile, the muscles in my face feeling alien and unresponsive.

[story 3]

The cheers of the other athletes sounded distant, muffled, like they were happening in another room. I stood near the edge of the field, watching the team assemble, the vibrant energy of them a stark contrast to the dull ache radiating from my core. My vision seemed to narrow, focusing only on the distance between me and the group. Every muscle in my body was coiled tight, ready for a blow that never quite landed. When the coach called out the final roster, the sound seemed to scrape against my nerves. When my name wasn't mentioned, a strange lightness washed over me, immediately replaced by a crushing weight. I took a deliberate, slow breath, trying to steady the tremor running through my limbs. I watched them jog onto the field, their camaraderie a visible, impenetrable wall, and I simply turned and walked toward the exit, each step feeling monumental and impossibly heavy.
